root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Workflow Reference Guide
root.summary: Front matter and full reference guide for Cúram 8.1.2 Workflow. It applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2 and maps the workflow manual’s main topics: process lifecycle and metadata, workflow data objects and enactment, activity types (automatic, event wait, manual, decision, subflow, loop, parallel), notifications, transitions, conditions, split/join, workflow structure, web service integration, file locations, runtime configuration/JMSLite, and inbox/task customization. The latter pages explain Guice-based customization of Inbox Actions, Task Actions, task search/query, and SQL generation, including the extension/binding pattern and module registration. The section also includes legal notices, permissions, warranties, export restrictions, sample code terms, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Brief introductory notice stating that before using the information or the supported product, readers should review the Notices section on page 135. Includes Merative US L.P. copyright dates (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Front matter for the Cúram Workflow Reference. States this edition applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2 and includes Merative copyright/trademark notices. The excerpt also contains the document contents and chapter roadmap: workflow process definitions, data flow, activities, flow control, development/runtime, and inbox customization, with subsections on metadata, enactment, activity types, transitions, workflow structure, web services, configuration, JMSLite, and inbox/task management.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Cúram Workflow Reference overview of process-definition lifecycle and metadata. Covers creating, visualizing, releasing, versioning, importing/exporting/copying process definitions in the PDT, plus required validations and localization rules. Explains workflow engine execution, latest released version behavior, and runtime records for activities/transitions. Also describes workflow data object templates and syncing from the template library. Includes process metadata fields such as id, version, language version, release status, category, display name, documentation, web-service exposure/callback, and failure allocation strategy.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Cúram Workflow Reference pages 25–34 cover workflow process validations, workflow data objects, context WDOs, runtime behavior, and process enactment. It explains required process metadata (unique name, English display name, category), WDO structure and XML metadata, attribute types and enactment requirements, and context objects like RuntimeInformation, Result, Event, Decision, Task, Loop, Deadline, Parallel, and Error. It also describes enactment mechanisms (code, event, subflow, web service) and enactment-mapping syntax.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: Covers workflow process enactment and activity metadata. Explains enactment mappings from Cúram structs to workflow data objects, including validation rules and examples, plus event-based enactment via ProcEnactmentEvt/ProcEnactEvtData tables and event fields. Introduces base activity metadata (id, category, name, description, localized text) and details automatic activities: bpo-mapping, interface/method validation, and input mappings for base type and struct parameters using workflow data objects.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: Covers workflow activity metadata for automatic activities: input mappings for aggregated structs, list structs, and indexed list items in loops, plus validation rules and runtime behavior. Also explains output mappings for primitive, struct, aggregated, and list return types, including required wdo-name/name attributes and restrictions. Defines Context_Result and Context_Error workflow data objects for transition conditions after automatic activities. Begins section 1.8 Event Wait, introducing event-wait activities, multiple events, wait-on-all-events behavior, and event metadata.

children.title: Edition — pages 55-64
children.summary: Cúram Workflow Reference pages 55–64 cover event wait activities, deadlines, output mappings, reminders, and the start of manual activities. It defines event wait metadata/validations, runtime behavior for waitOnAllEvents, deadline handlers and deadline output mappings, Context_Deadline and Context_Event WDOs, and reminder delivery rules/processing. It also introduces manual activities, task messages, and action metadata for user inbox tasks. Useful if you need workflow event/deadline configuration, mapping rules, or task notification setup.

children.title: Edition — pages 65-74
children.summary: This section documents manual activity task metadata and allocation strategies in Cúram Workflow. It explains task message text, localized parameters, action links, multiple-occurring actions, task priority, and initial comments, along with validation rules and page/action parameter requirements. It then covers manual task allocation strategies: function, classic rules, CER rules, and target, including their required metadata and mapping structures (function-mappings, ruleset-mappings, cer-set-mappings). Includes examples and notes on valid workflow data object usage.

children.title: Edition — pages 75-84
children.summary: Covers manual activity allocation strategy rules and runtime behavior: validation for target, function, classic rule, and CER rule allocation types; required work resolver interfaces/adapters; how workflow determines allocation targets, default work queue fallback, and auto-add-to-user-tasks behavior; and business object associations. Also introduces decision activities as a manual-activity specialization with parameterized subject/action messages, secondary actions, multiple-occurring actions, deadlines, and detailed metadata/validation rules for mappings, placeholders, and context WDO usage.

children.title: Edition — pages 85-94
children.summary: Cúram workflow reference covering decision activities, subflows, and loop begin/end. Decision activities support multiple-choice and free-text questions, with runtime task creation, DECISION.MADE events, context WDOs, metadata tags, and validation rules for placeholders, data types, and list/parallel context use. Subflows can run synchronously or asynchronously, with required input mappings and optional output mappings for synchronous cases, plus type/list constraints. Loop begin/end define while or do-while repetition, loop-exit conditions, nested-loop structure, and block-endpoint references.

children.title: Edition — pages 95-104
children.summary: Cúram Workflow Reference section covering loop runtime context, parallel activities, and notifications. It explains Context_Loop.loopCount for repeated loop iterations, then introduces parallel activities as wrappers for Manual and Decision activities driven by a list WDO to create multiple task instances at runtime, including metadata, examples, and validations. The notifications part defines notification subject/body/actions, allocation strategy, delivery mechanism, and multiple-occurring actions, with rules for using loop/parallel context variables and list WDO indexing, plus code APIs for resolving localized notification strings.

children.title: Edition — pages 105-114
children.summary: Cúram workflow reference covering notification delivery and transition/condition metadata. It explains how workflow notifications use an allocation strategy and a NotificationDelivery callback, with adapter/default classes and extendable delivery mechanisms via the DELIVERYMECHANISM code table. It also defines transitions as unidirectional links between activities, including required IDs, source/target constraints, validation rules, and optional conditions. Conditions are built from expressions using operators, conjunctions, brackets, and functions not() and isNothing(), with type and loop-related validation.

children.title: Edition — pages 115-124
children.summary: Covers workflow split metadata (XOR vs AND, transition order, idref) and the concepts/validation of workflow structure: graph properties, block structure, and loop/choice/parallel block rules. Also explains workflow web services for BPEL integration, including process enactment WSDL, callback port types, and partner links. Ends with file-location conventions for workflow process definition XMLs, import/build behavior, customization in the custom component, and a brief start on event definition files.

children.title: Edition — pages 125-134
children.summary: This section covers workflow configuration and Inbox/task management. It lists key Application.prx properties for workflow behavior, audit suppression, process cache size, batch launcher settings, and inbox list size limits. It explains JMSLite, a lightweight JMS server for IDE testing, its asynchronous behavior, and workflow debugging implications. It also details Inbox features: task lists, “Get Next Task” algorithms, task redirection/blocking security identifiers, and extensive customization points via Guice, including how to override InboxActions with a custom implementation and module binding.

Front matter and chapter roadmap for the Cúram Workflow Reference. It applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2 and includes copyright notices. The section outlines the full workflow manual: process definition lifecycle and metadata; workflow data objects and enactment; activity types (automatic, event wait, manual, decision, subflow, loop, parallel); notifications, transitions, conditions, split/join, and workflow structure; web service integration; file locations; runtime configuration/JMSLite; and Inbox/task management with customization via Guice.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section is mostly legal/front-matter content: sample data disclaimer, copyright license for sample programs, warranty/liability limits, a link to Merative’s privacy policy, and a trademarks notice listing Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX. It is useful for finding licensing terms, privacy-policy reference, and trademark attributions rather than product functionality.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: This section lists legal notices for the document, including fictitious examples, copyright/license terms for sample code, Merative privacy policy link, and trademark attributions. It identifies trademarks for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX, and notes that other names may be trademarks of their owners.

Legal notice and permissions for using the publication. Covers personal and commercial reproduction rules, requirement to preserve proprietary notices, prohibition on derivative works or external distribution without consent, and Merative’s right to withdraw permission. Includes warranty disclaimer (“as-is”), limits on liability and patent rights, export-law compliance, and a disclaimer about non-Merative products and external websites. Useful for locating licensing, usage restrictions, and legal disclaimers.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Workflow Reference. Lists chapter 1 and its subsections, covering workflow structure, process creation, process metadata and WDOs, enactment, activity types (automatic, event wait, manual, decision, subflow, loop, parallel), notifications, transitions, conditions, split/join, workflow structure rules, web services, file locations, configuration, JMSLite, and inbox/task management, plus front matter and legal notices. Useful as a navigation map to specific workflow topics and page ranges.

children.title: 1 Cúram Workflow Reference
1.1 Structure of this Document
Workflow Processes
children.summary: Introduces the Cúram Workflow Management System and explains that process definitions are the central component for defining business processes and their metadata. This section outlines the document’s workflow-related structure, highlighting topics covered in later sections: creating and visualizing workflow processes, releasing and versioning process definitions, importing/exporting, localization, running processes, and the method/template libraries. It also points to process definition metadata and workflow data objects as key related areas.

children.title: Data Flow
children.summary: Explains how data is stored and manipulated in a workflow process instance, especially how data enters from the outside world at enactment and moves between activities and transitions. This section helps determine how workflow runtime data is conveyed and managed within the Cúram Workflow Management System.

children.title: Activities
children.summary: Overview of workflow activities in Cúram WMS, describing activities as the steps where business processing occurs. It introduces the activity types covered in the guide and notes that notifications are relevant to each type. The section serves as a roadmap to Base Activity, Automatic, Event Wait, Manual, and Decision activities, each with metadata, validations, runtime behavior, and associated workflow data objects/mappings.

children.title: Flow Control
children.summary: Introduces Flow Control in Cúram WMS, explaining how information flow between workflow activities is specified and managed. Covers transitions between activities, condition metadata, split/join behavior, and workflow structure/block-structure constraints. Also references related flow-control concepts such as subflows, loops, parallels, and activity notifications, including their metadata, validations, and runtime behavior.

children.title: Development and Runtime
children.summary: Covers Cúram workflow development/runtime setup: how to run, configure, and debug workflows; web service exposure for process enactment; file locations for exported process definition and event source files; workflow application properties; and JMSLite for lightweight testing/debugging. Also introduces Inbox and Task Management configuration/customization in WMS, including task list display settings and Google Guice-based action customization, plus process creation, visualization, release/versioning, import/export, localization, and running processes with the workflow engine.

children.title: Inbox Configuration and Customization
children.summary: Covers Inbox and Task Management configuration in Cúram WMS. It explains how to control the number of tasks shown in Inbox-related lists and how to customize Inbox/Task Management actions using the Google Guice framework. Relevant if you need to tune inbox display behavior or alter available task-management functions.

This section is a roadmap to the Cúram Workflow Reference, explaining how the guide is organized around major WMS areas and what each later section covers. It previews workflow process metadata and lifecycle, data flow and workflow data objects, process enactment, activity types and notifications, flow control elements like transitions/conditions/splits/joins, and workflow structure constraints. Useful for locating the relevant part of the guide before diving into detailed metadata, validations, and runtime behavior.

children.title: 1.2 Creating a Workflow Process
Process definition lifecycle
Process creation
children.summary: Describes how workflow process definitions are created and managed in Cúram. Introduces the Process Definition Tool (PDT) for defining process behavior through activities and transitions, with support for maintaining definitions for the workflow engine. Notes PDT utilities for visualizing processes during design, plus copy, import, and export functions. Also explains the read-only graphical visualization that lets administrators inspect all activities, transitions, and possible execution paths in a process definition.

children.title: Process visualization
children.summary: Describes the Cúram Workflow Process Definition Tool’s read-only graphical visualization utility. It lets process administrators view process definitions while creating or modifying them, showing all activities and transitions and giving a high-level view of all possible execution paths. Useful for understanding or reviewing workflow structure before opening the node.

children.title: Releasing a process
children.summary: Explains releasing a process definition in PDT: a process must pass required validations before it can run in the workflow engine. Once released, it becomes read-only and any later edit creates a new unreleased version; only one unreleased version can exist at a time. Also notes process import/export/copy behavior: exported metadata can be reimported, and imported processes get the highest version number and remain unreleased regardless of prior state.

children.title: Process versions (process editing)
children.summary: Released processes are read-only in the PDT, so any attempt to edit one creates a new unreleased version. Only one unreleased version of a process can exist at a time; before editing a released process, any existing unreleased version must be released or deleted. This section explains process versioning during editing and why new versions are created for modifications.

children.title: Process import, export, and copy
Validations
children.summary: This section lists workflow process definition validation rules for import and copy operations, including uniqueness and name requirements, overwrite behavior, identifier consistency, maximum name lengths, valid code table references, and required English locale entries for all localizable text. It also introduces localization support in process definitions, explaining that metadata strings can be localized per user locale, with English required as a baseline and changes managed through the PDT localization screen.

Describes PDT process import, export, and copy behavior. Process definitions can be exported to the file system and imported into another system; imported processes always become the highest version and are unreleased, regardless of prior release state, so they must pass validations before use. Also notes that copying/editing a released process creates a new unreleased version, with only one unreleased version allowed at a time.

children.title: Localization
children.summary: Explains localization in workflow process definitions. Metadata text such as task subjects can be translated for different application locales using the Process Definition Tool. Localizable strings are identified via “Localized Text”; every localized entry must include an English (“en”) value, and PDT initially adds both the user’s locale and English. Later additions, deletions, or edits are done in the localization screen. This section introduces the list of text elements that can be localized.

Cúram workflow process definitions are created and administered in the Process Definition Tool (PDT) using activities and transitions. PDT provides read-only visualization of process structure, supports copy/import/export, and shows execution paths, activity details, notifications, split/join types, and transition conditions. A process must pass validations before it can be released to the workflow engine; once released, it is read-only and edits create a new unreleased version. Imported processes become the highest version and unreleased. The section also covers import/copy rules, name/identifier validations, and localization of process metadata text.

children.title: Process execution
Basic engine behavior
children.summary: Describes the workflow engine’s basic runtime role in Cúram Workflow Management System. It explains that a process definition is enacted by creating a process instance, which serves as the runtime data for the workflow. When enacted, the engine checks the relevant database table and uses the latest released version of the specified process definition to start the instance. Useful for understanding how workflow execution begins and which process version is run.

children.title: Executing multiple versions
children.summary: Released process versions do not affect running instances: each process continues to completion using the version it started with. The section also introduces workflow process instance administration, describing how an administrator can suspend, resume, or abort running instances and how these actions affect active activities, manual/decision tasks, and synchronous subflows.

children.title: Process Instance Administration
children.summary: Explains workflow process instance administration. Describes how the engine creates activity and transition instance records as a process runs, and notes that released new versions do not affect already running instances. Details admin controls in the Workflow Administration interface: suspend, resume, and abort process instances, including effects on active activities, synchronous subflows, and manual/decision tasks. Also introduces the Method Reference Library, which exposes application business process/entity methods for workflow use.

Introduces workflow process execution in Cúram: a process definition must be enacted as a process instance, which is the runtime data for execution. Describes the workflow engine as the runtime environment and notes that, when a process is enacted, it checks the relevant database table and starts the instance using the latest released version of the specified process definition.

children.title: Method Reference Library
Referencing Cúram methods
children.summary: This section explains how business process object/entity methods are added to the Method Reference Library before use in process definitions, and how the chosen method type controls where each method can be used. It covers three types: General (automatic activities), Allocation (allocation strategies for manual/decision/parallel activities and notifications; must return AllocationTargetList), and Deadline (deadline handlers for event-wait/manual/decision/parallel activities). It also notes that removing a reference does not invalidate existing process definitions if the method remains valid.

children.title: Method types
children.summary: Explains the Method Reference Library and method types used in Cúram process definitions. Methods must be added to the library before use, and removing a reference does not invalidate existing process definitions if the method remains valid. Describes three method types: General (startable only from automatic activities), Allocation (usable in allocation strategies for manual/decision/parallel activities and notifications; must return AllocationTargetList), and Deadline (usable as deadline handlers for event-wait, manual, decision, and parallel activities). Includes a brief transition to WDO templates.

Explains the Method Reference Library, which lets workflow designers add selected BPO/entity methods for use in process definitions instead of exposing all application methods. The section introduces method types that control where a method can be used: General for automatic activities, Allocation for allocation strategies and notifications (must return AllocationTargetList), and Deadline for deadline handlers on event-wait/manual/decision/parallel activities. It also notes that removing a reference does not invalidate existing process definitions if the method remains valid.

children.title: WDO templates
Metadata
children.summary: Explains the Method Reference Library and WDO templates. It says BPO/entity methods must be added to the method library before use in process definitions, and removing a reference does not invalidate existing definitions if the method remains valid. It defines method types: General (automatic activities), Allocation (manual/decision/parallel activities and notifications; must return AllocationTargetList), and Deadline (deadline handlers for event-wait, manual, decision, and parallel activities). Also notes the workflow data object template library lets reusable WDOs be imported instead of recreated.

children.title: Import and syncing
children.summary: Explains importing and syncing workflow data object templates in process definitions. Imported templates from the workflow data object template library add the object and all attributes to the current process definition. Templates can later be synchronized with the library entry at any time, forcing the process definition’s template name and display name to match the library. This section follows metadata details and notes template storage in the WDOTemplateLibrary table.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: Validates workflow data object import/synchronization from a template library: if a workflow data object with the same name already exists in the process definition, it cannot be imported. The surrounding text also notes that synchronizing from a template can add new attributes automatically, and optionally override existing ones, which may invalidate the process definition. This section sits within process definition metadata, describing top-level process information such as id, version, name, description, and failure allocation strategy.

Explains the Method Reference Library and workflow data object (WDO) template library. BPO/entity methods must be added to the method library before use in process definitions; removing a reference does not invalidate existing definitions if the method remains valid. Method types are General (automatic activities), Allocation (manual/decision/parallel activities and notifications; must return AllocationTargetList), and Deadline (deadline handlers). WDO templates let reusable workflow data objects be imported into process definitions and synchronized later; template metadata is stored in WDOTemplateLibrary, with some WDO fields fixed to defaults.

Cúram workflow processes are created in PDT as activities and transitions, with read-only visualization of paths, notifications, split/join types, and transition conditions. Processes must pass validations before release; released definitions are read-only and edits create a new version. Import/export/copy rules, naming and localization requirements, and execution as a process instance in the workflow engine are covered. The section also explains the Method Reference Library for restricting BPO/entity methods by type, and WDO templates for reusable, syncable workflow data objects.

children.title: 1.3 Process Definition Metadata
Metadata
children.summary: Defines process definition metadata in Cúram workflow. Covers the `workflow-process` parent tag and fields such as id, process-version, language-version, released, category, createdBy, creationDate, process-display-name, name, description, documentation, web-service/expose/callback-service, and failure-allocation-strategy/allocation-strategy. Explains which fields are for identification, versioning, localization, search, enactment, web service exposure, and allocation fallback behavior. Notes key validations and uniqueness rules, including unique process id/name and released versions only being enactable.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists workflow process validation rules: process names must be unique and nonempty, English display name and category are required, and deleted released versions are restricted when enacted or referenced by latest released subflows. It also validates failure allocation strategy type, and callback service class requirements when a process is exposed as a web service. The section then introduces workflow data objects and list workflow data objects, explaining their lifecycle, use in activities/transitions, and the need to populate attributes before use to avoid runtime failures.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: Explains that certain generic runtime context must be available to workflow activities and transitions through a Context_RuntimeInformation workflow data object, with details referenced elsewhere. Introduces workflow data objects and list workflow data objects as named, typed process-defined data containers used throughout a process instance’s lifetime. Notes they are created at enactment, can be populated by enactment or activity outputs, and must be initialized before use or runtime failures occur. Also indicates the section leads into metadata and validation rules for WDO creation and modification.

Defines workflow process definition metadata in Cúram. Covers the `workflow-process` parent tag and key fields: id, process-version, language-version, released, category, createdBy, creationDate, process-display-name, name, description, documentation, web-service/expose/callback-service, and failure-allocation-strategy/allocation-strategy. Explains identification, versioning, localization, search-only fields, enactment rules, web service exposure, and fallback task allocation behavior. Key validations include unique process id/name, released-only enactment/subflow use, and failure allocation strategy type TARGET.

children.title: 1.4 Workflow Data Objects
Metadata
children.summary: Defines metadata for workflow process data objects and their attributes in Cúram workflow definitions. Explains WDO elements such as is-list-wdo, initialize-attributes, wdo-name, display-name, description, and attributes, plus attribute metadata like attribute-name, display-name, type, required-at-enactment, process-output, and constant-value. Includes examples of a task-creation WDO and a child-details list WDO, and notes how these settings affect enactment mappings, data compatibility, default initialization, and output to calling BPEL processes.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists workflow validation rules for data objects and attributes. It covers uniqueness and naming constraints, reserved prefixes/names, required presence of at least one attribute, valid Cúram base domain types, and restrictions on constants and initial values. It also states that an attribute cannot be both required for enactment and constant, or both constant and initial-value, and that constant/initial values must be type-compatible; blank values are only allowed for STRING attributes.

children.title: List of Context WDOs
children.summary: Defines context workflow data objects (WDOs) used by the workflow engine and Process Definition Tool during process execution. It explains that these are not explicitly defined in process metadata, and briefly describes Context_RuntimeInformation (processInstanceID, enactingUser, enactmentTime), Context_Result (runtime return value persisted for outbound transition conditions), Context_Event (event details for transitions from event-wait activities), and Context_Decision (attributes depend on decision answer format).

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains runtime workflow data objects available during process execution. Covers Context_Task, Context_Loop, Context_Deadline, Context_Parallel, and Context_Error: when each is available, what data they expose, and how they are used in mappings, conditions, parameters, and transitions. Also states that workflow data object instances exist from process enactment until completion, making them available throughout the process lifetime. Notes that Context_RuntimeInformation.enactingUser is set to the username of the user whose actions triggered the workflow.

Defines workflow data objects (WDOs) and list WDOs in Cúram workflow processes: metadata elements, attribute fields, enactment mappings, initialization, constants, initial values, and process-output behavior. Includes examples of a task-details WDO and a child-details list WDO. Also lists validation rules for unique/named WDOs and attributes, reserved names/prefixes, required attributes, type compatibility, and constraints on constants/initial values and webservice output. Describes context WDOs such as Context_RuntimeInformation, Context_Result, Context_Event, Context_Decision, Context_Task, Context_Loop, Context_Deadline, Context_Parallel, and Context_Error, plus their runtime availability.

children.title: 1.5 Process Enactment
Code enactment (enactment service API)
Metadata
children.summary: Describes XML metadata for enactment mappings used by the enactment service API. It shows how specific Cúram struct attributes are mapped to process WDO attributes as initial data when starting a process instance. Key elements include `enactment-mappings`, `mapping`, and mandatory `source-attribute`, with examples of source-to-target attribute pairs. Notes that enactment mappings are optional for a process definition, but can provide data needed to enact the process.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section defines validations for enactment mappings in Cúram workflows. It states that the source struct attribute must be valid and compatible in type with the target workflow data object attribute, and the target attribute must be valid and marked required for enactment. If the target is in a list workflow data object, the source must come from a list struct field. It appears alongside terminology for struct names, field names, target attributes, and workflow data object attributes.

children.title: Code
children.summary: Explains enactment mapping fields for Cúram workflow metadata: struct-name, name, target-attribute, wdo-name, and required workflow data object attributes. Covers validations for source/target attribute types, required enactment flags, and the rule that list workflow data objects must be mapped from list struct fields. Includes sample code showing how to build enactment structs using a flat struct, an aggregated struct, and a list struct for workflow enactment.

Explains the enactment service API for starting a workflow process directly from application code, including its drawback of hardcoded logic that requires redeployment for changes. Defines enactment-mappings metadata: mappings from Cúram struct attributes to workflow data object attributes, with mandatory source-attribute, target-attribute, struct-name, and wdo-name. Notes mappings are optional but can provide initial process data. Covers validation rules for valid/type-compatible source and required target attributes, including list WDOs requiring list struct fields, and shows sample flat, aggregated, and list struct code.

children.title: Event enactment
Configuration data
children.summary: Describes event-driven workflow enactment configuration. An event-to-process association is stored in ProcEnactmentEvt, specifying event class/type, target process, and whether it is enabled; the latest released process version is enacted. Related mapping data is stored in ProcEnactEvtData, linking event fields to workflow data object attributes required by the process. Configuration can be managed at runtime via UI or database entries, but only for processes needing limited enactment data.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: Defines validation rules for event-based workflow enactment. Event data must fully populate a process definition’s enactment data; if a process already uses event-based enactment, later enactment-data changes must remain compatible with existing event mappings. A process enacted from an event cannot delete its latest released version if the next latest released version cannot be fully populated from that event.

Explains event-based workflow enactment. A raised event can start a process if ProcEnactmentEvt defines the event class/type, target process, and enabled flag; the latest released process version is always used. ProcEnactEvtData maps event fields to required workflow data object attributes, and every required enactment mapping must be fully populated. Configuration can be managed at runtime via UI or database, but is best suited to processes needing only limited enactment data.

Explains process enactment, i.e., starting a workflow process instance, and the required initial enactment data. Covers two main mechanisms: code enactment via the EnactmentService API and event enactment via configured event-process mappings. Code enactment uses enactment-mappings metadata to map Cúram struct attributes to required workflow data object attributes, with validation rules for type/field compatibility and list handling. Event enactment uses ProcEnactmentEvt and ProcEnactEvtData tables, is runtime-configurable, and always starts the latest released process version.

children.title: 1.6 Base Activity
Metadata
Localized Text
children.summary: This section explains localized text for workflow activity metadata. It defines the localized-text element used for names and descriptions, requiring at least one locale child. It notes that localizable text is saved under both the user’s locale and English, and that locale is identified by language plus optional country (e.g., en, en_US, en_GB are distinct). It also covers validation: activity names must be mandatory, unique within a workflow definition, and unique per locale, with activity type constraints mentioned.

Cúram workflow activity metadata defines the common base details for all activity types: each activity has a localizable name and optional localizable description used by the workflow engine and Process Definition Tool. It introduces the localized-text structure with locale children, where locale is language plus optional country (for example, en, en_US). Text is stored for the user’s locale and English. Activity names must be mandatory, unique within a workflow definition, and unique per locale, with activity type-specific constraints.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists validation rules for workflow activity metadata. It states that activity names are mandatory, must be unique within a workflow definition, and must also be unique per locale because names are localizable. It also notes that an activity must be one of the permitted activity types, though this is usually enforced by the Process Definition Tool.

children.title: Basic Activity Types
Route Activity
children.summary: Route activities do no business work and do not change application data; they serve as null flow-control points, commonly used for splits, joins, and to handle processes that do not fit valid block structure. They can also carry notifications, effectively acting as standalone notifications. The section also notes that every process must have exactly one start-process and one end-process activity, which are auto-created by the Process Definition Tool and form the outer block of a valid workflow.

children.title: Start/End Process Activity
children.summary: Explains start-process and end-process activities as the process’s begin/end markers and anchor points for transitions. A valid process must contain exactly one of each, and all transitions from start should lead to end, though runtime paths may vary. These activities are auto-created by the Process Definition Tool and cannot be manually created. They form the outermost block of a valid block-structured workflow.

This section introduces basic activity types in Cúram workflow: route and start/end-process. Route activities do no business work, do not change application data, and are used for flow control such as splits, joins, block-structure workarounds, or standalone notifications. Start-process and end-process mark a process’s boundaries and anchor transitions; a valid process must have exactly one of each, created automatically by the Process Definition Tool and not manually editable.

Base Activity defines common metadata for all Cúram workflow activities: a required, localizable name and optional localizable description, plus activity id and optional category. It explains the localized-text format (locale children with language and optional country; text stored for user locale and English) and notes that locale strings like en, en_US, and en_GB are distinct. Validations require activity names to be mandatory, unique within a workflow definition, and unique per locale, and activities must be of a permitted type.

children.title: 1.7 Automatic
Prerequisites
children.summary: This section explains prerequisites for Cúram automatic activities. It notes a limitation: automatic activities cannot call BPO methods using Java 8 constructs. It also states that automatic activities use the same base details as other activity types, then introduces how they work by starting Cúram business methods (BPO or entity methods). The section highlights the key metadata mapping, especially the mandatory `bpo-mapping`, `interface-name`, and `method-name` attributes used to identify the method invoked by the automatic activity.

children.title: Cúram Business Methods
Metadata
children.summary: Defines the metadata for a Cúram automatic activity that invokes a business method. It explains that automatic activities run Cúram business methods (BPO or entity methods), and that the critical configuration is the method to start plus its parameters. The key XML element is `bpo-mapping`, which specifies the target interface and method, along with input/output mappings. Mandatory attributes covered here are `interface-name` (fully qualified interface name) and `method-name` (method to run).

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section defines validations and input-mapping rules for automatic activity business process object methods in Cúram workflows. It requires both interface and method names, with the interface class existing on the application classpath and the method existing on that interface. It explains that failures in automatic activity methods normally trigger workflow error handling, retries, and possible transition evaluation via Context_Error. It also introduces input mappings for method parameters, including metadata such as formal-parameters, formal-parameter, and zero-based index, with base types requiring mappings.

children.title: Code
children.summary: Explains automatic activity code mapping in Cúram workflows. Any public business method (BPO or entity) can be invoked by an automatic activity; failures typically trigger Workflow Error Handling, retries, and modeled-exception transitions using Context_Error for remedial paths. Describes input mappings: workflow data object attributes supply method parameters, with primitive base types requiring mappings and struct fields optional. Also defines shared mapping metadata such as formal-parameters, formal-parameter, and zero-based index.

Defines the core metadata for Cúram automatic activities that invoke business methods. Automatic activities run public Cúram BPO or entity methods, and the key configuration is the `bpo-mapping` element, which identifies the target interface and method plus input/output mappings. It highlights mandatory attributes `interface-name` (fully qualified interface) and `method-name` (method to run), and notes a limitation: automatic activities cannot target BPO methods using Java 8 constructs due to workflow mapping constraints.

children.title: Input Mappings
Metadata
Input mappings for base type parameters
children.summary: Explains automatic activity business method input mappings for base type parameters. It defines how workflow data object attributes populate method parameters, notes that primitive base type parameters must be mapped, and that mapping metadata includes formal-parameters, formal-parameter, and zero-based index. Also covers validation and error handling for automatic activity method execution, including retry behavior and workflow error handling for failed methods.

children.title: Input mappings for struct parameters
children.summary: Explains how automatic activity business method parameters of struct type are mapped from workflow data objects. Shows XML examples of `<bpo-mapping>`, `<formal-parameters>`, `<formal-parameter>`, `<struct>`, and nested `<field>` elements. Defines the mandatory `type` attribute for the struct as the fully qualified struct name, and `name` plus nested `base-type`/`wdo-attribute` mappings for each field. Notes that fields map to primitive base types and that an input mapping may be omitted for a parameter.

children.title: Input mappings for aggregated struct parameters
children.summary: Explains input mappings for aggregated struct parameters in business method metadata. It extends the standard struct parameter mapping pattern by allowing a struct field to resolve to another struct rather than a primitive. In that case, the field name refers to the role in the struct association, not the mapped field name. Includes an example `bpo-mapping` for `createBenefit` showing nested struct fields (`PlannedItemDetails` containing `PlannedItemKey`) and `wdo-attribute` mappings.

children.title: Input mappings for list struct parameters
children.summary: Explains how to define input mappings for list structure parameters in workflow BPO mappings. The metadata is like aggregate struct mappings, but the struct type is the list structure name, and the first field names the role of the child struct within the association. The mapped workflow data object must be a list workflow data object. Includes an XML example mapping a `ClaimantDependentDetailsList` to nested `ClaimantDependentDetails` fields such as identifier, firstName, and surname.

children.title: Input mappings and indexed items from list workflow data objects
children.summary: Explains how list workflow data objects are used in input mappings for activities inside loops. Each loop iteration can map the next list item to formal parameter fields using `Context_Loop.loopCount` in the `wdo-name`, with an XML example showing struct field mappings. Also lists validation rules: workflow data object attributes must be valid and type-compatible, `Context_Task` is only for manual/decision activities, and `Context_Loop` only for activities contained within a loop.

Defines metadata for automatic activity business method input mappings. Covers common elements like formal-parameters, formal-parameter, and zero-based index, plus validation that interface and method names must exist. Explains base type mappings (primitive parameters must be mapped), struct mappings, aggregated struct mappings where fields can resolve to nested structs, and list struct mappings using list workflow data objects. Also notes automatic activity method failures trigger workflow error handling/retry, and modeled exceptions can drive alternate transitions via Context_Error.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: Covers validations for input mappings using list workflow data objects in looped activities. Notes that loop iterations use `Context_Loop.loopCount` to select the correct list item for formal parameter population. Valid workflow data object attributes must meet general attribute rules, mapped types must be compatible, `Context_Task` is only allowed for manual or decision activities, and `Context_Loop` is only allowed when the activity is inside a loop.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains runtime behavior for workflow activity parameter mappings. Input mappings pass workflow data object attribute values to a business method before an automatic activity starts, with validation rules for base types, structs, list structures, and indexed list items in loops. Also covers output mappings: automatic activities can write method return values back to workflow data objects. Notes support for primitive, struct, aggregated struct, and list struct return types, with example metadata snippets for primitive returns.

Defines workflow input mappings for automatic activity business method parameters. Covers required interface/method validation, failure handling, and mapping workflow data object attributes to primitive, struct, aggregated struct, and list struct parameters. Explains metadata elements like formal-parameters, formal-parameter, index, base-type, struct, field, and wdo-attribute. Also describes loop-specific mappings using Context_Loop.loopCount to select list items. Validates type compatibility, WDO attribute validity, and limits on Context_Task and Context_Loop usage.

children.title: Output Mappings
Metadata
Primitive return type
children.summary: This section explains output mappings for automatic activities when the business process object method returns a primitive type. It notes that output mappings let workflow data objects be populated from method results, but for a primitive return type only one return mapping entry can be specified. It also frames output mappings as applicable to primitive, struct, aggregated struct, and list struct return types, with this subsection focusing on the primitive case and showing an XML metadata example.

children.title: Struct return type
children.summary: Shows how an automatic activity maps a BPO method return to a struct. The example for `createAssociatedProductDeliveryForPlannedItem` returns a struct with fields `description` and `subject`, each populated from `SPProductDeliveryPI` wdo-attributes. The section introduces struct return types and begins an aggregated struct return type example.

children.title: Aggregated struct return type
children.summary: Explains how an automatic activity’s BPO mapping can return an aggregated struct rather than a single value. The example maps `createAssociatedProductDeliveryForPlannedItem` to a `<struct>` return containing multiple fields, such as `description` and `subject`, each sourced from WDO attributes. Useful for understanding struct-based return value mapping in Cúram workflow automatic activities.

children.title: List struct return type
children.summary: Shows an example of a list-style struct return mapping for `readClaimantDependentDetails` in `curam.sample.facade.intf.SampleBenefit`. The excerpt illustrates a `<return>` containing a `<struct>` with nested `dtls` fields, including `identifier` and `firstName` mapped from `ClaimantDependent` attributes. Useful for understanding how return structs are defined and how nested fields are bound in BPO mappings.

This section documents output metadata for automatic activity BPO mappings. It explains how workflow data object values are populated from method returns and distinguishes primitive, struct, aggregated struct, and list struct return types. It notes primitive returns allow only one return mapping, while struct-based returns can map one or more fields. Examples show XML mappings for `createAssociatedProductDeliveryForPlannedItem` and `readClaimantDependentDetails`, including nested `dtls` fields and WDO attribute bindings.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: Defines output mapping validation rules for automatic activities: no duplicate output parameter mappings are allowed, meaning each workflow data object attribute may appear only once in the output return mappings list. Also explains the meaning of return/struct/field/base-type/wdo-attribute elements used in output mappings, including primitive, struct, aggregated struct, and list struct return types.

children.title: Runtime information
children.summary: Explains runtime behavior for automatic activity output mappings and context workflow data objects. Output mapping values are persisted into workflow data object attributes after the activity runs; mapped attributes must exist, have compatible types, and cannot be constant. List return structs require a list workflow data object. Describes Context_Result for transition conditions: available for base types, structs, and nested structs (with colon-separated nested field names), but not for list structs. Introduces Context_Error for handling failures from BPO methods called by automatic activities.

Defines automatic activity output mappings for business process object methods. Covers how returned values populate workflow data object attributes for primitive, struct, aggregated struct, and list struct returns, with XML examples. Explains mapping elements such as return, struct, field, base-type, and wdo-attribute, including nested field names and role-based names for aggregated/list structs. Notes validations like no duplicate output mappings and one mapping per workflow data object attribute. Also describes runtime persistence behavior and Context_Result/Context_Error use in transitions.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: This section explains the context workflow data objects available for conditions on transitions from automatic activities. It details Context_Result, which exposes the started method’s return value for use in data item/function conditions: base types use value, structs expose their fields, nested structs use both top-level and fully qualified colon-separated field names, and list structs do not support Context_Result. It also introduces Context_Error for modeling follow-on actions when an automatic activity fails and rolls back.

Describes Cúram automatic activities, which run fully automated business methods with no human intervention. Covers prerequisites and a key limitation: they cannot call BPO methods using Java 8 constructs. Explains core `bpo-mapping` metadata, including mandatory `interface-name` and `method-name`, plus input mappings from workflow data objects to base, struct, aggregated struct, and list struct parameters. Also covers output mappings from return values back to workflow data objects, validations, runtime persistence, and context WDOs such as `Context_Result` and `Context_Error` for transition conditions and failure handling.

children.title: 1.8 Event Wait
Prerequisites
children.summary: Prerequisites for Cúram Workflow event wait activities: they use the common Base Activity details described in section 1.6. The section introduces event wait behavior, noting that an activity can wait for one or more events, and can complete on the first matching event unless configured to require all specified events.

children.title: List of events
Metadata
children.summary: Defines metadata for `event-wait-activity` and nested `event-wait` configuration. Explains `wait-on-all-events`: when `false`, the first matching event completes the activity; when `true`, all specified events must be raised before the workflow continues. Shows example XML for waiting on Task Close, Parent Approve, and multiple Child Approve events, including event matching via `event-match-attribute` and `multiple-occurring-event` with `list-wdo-name`.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists validation rules for event wait activities. It requires at least one event, valid event class/type database entries, and only one use of the same event class/type/match-attribute combination per activity. The mapped event-match workflow data object attribute must be valid and of type LONG. It also restricts use of Context_Task to manual or parallel manual activities (non-multiple events), and specifies when indexed list or parallel list workflow data objects may be used as event match data.

children.title: Code
children.summary: Describes workflow event handling in Cúram: event waits can be registered per activity instance, with waitOnAllEvents controlling whether one match or all matches are needed. The Workflow Event Handler completes activities, advances the process, removes matched waits, and persists output mappings for later use. Runtime behavior requires the raised event to match both the waited-for event and its primary event data. Also introduces deadlines for event waits to avoid indefinite blocking when an expected event may never occur.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Describes workflow event wait runtime behavior. A raised event only advances a process if it matches the waiting event and the specified event match attribute equals the event’s primary data. Covers multiple event waits per activity, including waitOnAllEvents false (first matching wait completes the activity and removes the others) versus true (all waits must match before progression). Notes output mappings are persisted. Also introduces deadlines to prevent indefinite waiting when an expected event may never occur.

Defines event-wait activity metadata, validation rules, and runtime behavior. An event wait can watch multiple events; with wait-on-all-events=false, the first matching event completes the activity, while true requires all specified events. Events use event-class, event-type, identifier, and event-match-attribute, with optional multiple-occurring-event/list-wdo-name for repeated waits. Validations require at least one valid event, unique class/type/match combinations, LONG match attributes, and restrictions on Context_Task and indexed/parallel list WDO usage.

children.title: Deadline
Prerequisites
children.summary: This section states that deadline handler methods for event wait deadlines must be Cúram business process object methods. It warns that workflow infrastructure limitations prevent deadline handler functions from referring to BPO methods that use Java 8 constructs. It also points readers to the “Automatic” section for details on input mappings and metadata for formal parameters. The page includes an XML metadata example showing an event-wait activity with a deadline handler, parameter mappings, and deadline output mappings.

children.title: Metadata
children.summary: This section shows the XML metadata for an event-wait activity deadline. It includes a complete-activity deadline, a mapped duration from TaskCreateDetails.deadlineDuration, a deadline-handler definition (interface curam.core.sl.intf.WorkflowDeadlineFunction, method defaultDeadlineHandler), formal-parameter mappings to TaskKey and ChildKey structs, and output mappings for duration-expired and deadline-expiry-time. It also notes that deadline handler methods must be Cúram business process object methods and cannot use BPO methods containing Java 8 constructs.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: Validation rules for workflow deadline handlers and input mappings. If a deadline handler is specified, it must refer to a valid Cúram business method on the classpath. Input-mapped workflow data object attributes must be valid and type-compatible with the target formal parameters. Indexed list items like ClaimantDependent[Context_Loop.loopCount] may only be used when the source data object is a list and the activity is inside a loop.

children.title: Code
children.summary: This section covers workflow deadline behavior and validation rules. It explains how deadlines are defined using seconds or a workflow data object attribute, how deadline handlers and complete-activity flags interact, and restrictions for parallel activities and mappings. It also notes required data types for deadline-related attributes and that handler return values are ignored. Finally, it describes runtime processing via the ScanTaskDeadlines batch job and DeadlineScanner.scanDeadlines(), including how expired deadlines trigger handlers, complete activities, and persist deadline outputs/context data.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains runtime handling of workflow deadlines. When an activity with deadline metadata runs, the engine computes the deadline from either seconds or an INTEGER workflow data object attribute. Expired deadlines are processed by the ScanTaskDeadlines batch job via DeadlineScanner.scanDeadlines(), which starts the handler method and/or completes the activity, and persists deadline output mappings and Context_Deadline data for transitions. Also notes constraints: parallel activities cannot use complete-activity deadlines, return parameters on deadline handlers are ignored, and deadline output mappings must be BOOLEAN/DATETIME where applicable.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: Explains Context_Deadline workflow data object usage in data item/function conditions, especially for event-wait activities with deadlines. It exposes two attributes: Context_Deadline.durationExpired (boolean if the deadline has expired) and Context_Deadline.expiryTime (deadline expiry date/time). Also describes event output mappings, showing how secondary event data, the actual raised event, and the raising user can be mapped back into the workflow engine; mappings are processed each time a matching event is raised when waiting for all associated events.

Defines event-wait deadlines in workflows: how to specify a deadline duration (seconds or mapped INTEGER WDO), deadline handler metadata and input mappings, and optional complete-activity behavior. Notes handler methods must be Cúram BPO methods and cannot use Java 8 constructs. Covers validations for valid/type-compatible mappings, loop/parallel restrictions, and output mappings to BOOLEAN/DATETIME fields. Explains runtime processing via ScanTaskDeadlines and DeadlineScanner.scanDeadlines(), which trigger handlers, complete activities, and persist deadline/context data when deadlines expire.

children.title: Output Mappings
Metadata
children.summary: Defines workflow metadata for event wait output mappings and the Context_Deadline WDO. Context_Deadline can be used in data item or function conditions and exposes durationExpired and expiryTime. The section explains that event output mappings can pass secondary event data, the actual raised event type, the user who raised it, and raise time back into the workflow engine. If an activity waits on multiple events, mappings are processed for each matching event. Includes an XML example for <event-output-mappings>.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: Defines validation rules for event wait output mappings in Cúram workflows: event type and raised-by mappings must point to STRING data object attributes, output data to LONG, and time raised to DATETIME. Also explains runtime population of mapped workflow data when an event is raised, lists Context_Event WDO attributes available in conditions, and notes that reminders can be attached to deadlines on manual/decision/event wait/parallel activities using notification metadata.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Describes runtime behavior for event wait activities: event output mappings populate workflow data object attributes when the awaited event is raised and are persisted. Lists valid mappings and types for event type, raised-by user name, output data, and time raised. Introduces the Context_Event WDO for conditions, exposing raisedByUserName, timeRaised, fullyQualifiedEventType, and outputData. Also notes reminders can be set on deadlines for several activity types and use standard notification metadata.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: Defines the Context_Event workflow data object used in data item or function conditions for transitions from event-wait activities. Lists available attributes: raisedByUserName, timeRaised, fullyQualifiedEventType, and outputData, with their meanings. Also notes that event output mappings populate and persist workflow data object attributes when the waited-on event is raised, with type validations: STRING for event type and raised-by, LONG for output data, and DATETIME for time raised.

Defines event wait output mappings in workflows. Raised events can map secondary event data, the actual event type, the user who raised it, and the raise time back into the workflow engine; mappings are processed for each matching event when multiple waits exist. Includes XML metadata for `<event-output-mappings>`. The section also introduces `Context_Deadline` for deadline conditions, exposing `durationExpired` and `expiryTime`.

children.title: Reminders
Metadata
children.summary: Defines XML metadata for deadline reminders. Shows `<reminders>` containing one or more `<reminder>` elements with `id`, `delivery-offset` (from ReminderDeliveryOffset), `delivery-time` specified either as `seconds` or `mapped-delivery-time`, and associated notification metadata. Explains each tag’s meaning: reminders are optional, delivery offset is relative to deadline expiry, and mapped delivery time references a workflow data object attribute. Lists validations: a deadline must exist with handler set and complete-activity true; reminder IDs must be unique; one of seconds or mapped-delivery-time is required; seconds must precede expiry; mapped attribute must be INTEGER.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists validation rules for reminder metadata on deadlines. It explains that reminders can only be created when an activity has an associated deadline with a handler set and the complete-activity indicator enabled. Reminder IDs must be unique per deadline. A reminder must specify either seconds or a mapped-delivery-time; seconds must be before the deadline expiry, and any workflow data object used for mapped-delivery-time must be INTEGER.

children.title: Code
children.summary: Explains reminder processing for workflow deadlines: `DeadlineScanner.scanDeadlines()` calls `deliverReminders()` to send reminders when due. Reminder send time is calculated from the deadline duration minus the reminder delivery duration, stored as a due datetime on the Reminders entity. The `ScanTaskDeadlines` batch job runs the scanner, sends notifications via the reminder allocation strategy, removes sent reminders, and clears unsent ones when the activity ends. Also notes that manual activities model human intervention in workflow and define notification details and agent assignment.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains runtime behavior for workflow reminders. When an activity with reminders runs, reminders are stored on the Reminders entity and their due time is calculated from the deadline duration minus the reminder delivery duration, then saved as a due date/time. The ScanTaskDeadlines batch job invokes DeadlineScanner.scanDeadlines(), which delivers due reminders and removes sent ones; unsent reminders are removed when the activity completes. Also notes that reminder notification validations match those for activity notifications.

Defines reminder metadata and rules for workflow deadlines. Reminders can be attached to manual, decision, event wait, parallel manual, or parallel decision activities, using standard notification metadata. XML supports `<reminders>` with one or more `<reminder>` elements, each with a unique id, `delivery-offset` (relative to deadline expiry), and `delivery-time` via either `seconds` or `mapped-delivery-time` (INTEGER WDO attribute). Reminders require an existing deadline with handler set and complete-activity true.

Explains Cúram Workflow event wait activities, where a branch pauses until one or more business events occur. Covers metadata for waiting on multiple events, wait-on-all-events behavior, event matching via class/type/identifier and workflow data object attributes, and restrictions on list/parallel/context use. Also describes deadline support with handlers, input/output mappings, scanner runtime behavior, and Context_Deadline. Includes event output mappings for secondary data, raiser, event type, and time, plus reminder metadata and validation rules for deadlines.

children.title: 1.9 Manual
Prerequisites
children.summary: This prerequisites section says manual activities inherit the common base activity details described in “Base Activity” and those base rules apply to manual activities. It introduces manual activities as workflow steps requiring human intervention, used for decisions, additional data, or real-world actions, and notes they define both the notification information sent to users and how agents are selected for assignment.

children.title: Task details
Metadata
children.summary: Explains task metadata for manual activity tasks in Cúram Workflow. Covers task messages and parameterized subject text with replaceable placeholders and formatting codes, localized text, message parameters, actions, link parameters, multiple-occurring actions, and task priority. Also describes whether actions are principal or secondary, modal vs non-modal opening, and how priorities can be set or mapped from workflow data objects. Includes an XML example showing these elements in context.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists validation rules for manual activity task mappings. It covers required subject text, validity of workflow data object attributes used in subject/action text and action links, restrictions on indexed list values and Context_Parallel usage, placeholder counts matching mapped attributes across locales, and priority configuration rules (codetable vs workflow data object, with STRING type required for mapped priority).

children.title: Code
children.summary: This section lists key code-related APIs and validation rules for manual activity tasks in Cúram Workflow. It covers valid action pages and parameters, locale-based task string resolution via LocalizableStringResolver.TaskStringResolver, TaskAdmin, TaskHistoryAdmin, and WorkflowDeadlineAdmin APIs, plus runtime behavior when a manual activity starts and creates an assigned task. It also notes constraints on initial comment mappings and multiple-occurring action list workflow data objects.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: This section outlines runtime requirements and related APIs for manual activity tasks in Cúram Workflow. It covers validation rules for comment mappings, multiple-occurring action list data, and action link parameters, plus notes on valid action pages. It also references APIs for resolving localized task strings, administering tasks, task history, and workflow deadlines. Finally, it explains that when a manual activity runs, the workflow engine creates a task and allocates it to an agent.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: This section describes context WDOs and allocation strategy metadata for manual activities. It explains that Context_Task exposes Context_Task.taskID, the unique ID of the task created when a manual activity runs, and that this ID can be used in event match and deadline mappings. It also outlines supported allocation strategy types for manual work assignment: function, classic rules, CER rules, and target, with notes on function-based prerequisites and a limitation on Java 8 constructs in BPO methods.

Defines manual activity task details in Cúram Workflow: inbox task message/subject metadata, localized parameterized text with placeholders and formatting, action links to application pages, link parameters, multiple-occurring actions, task priority, and initial comments. Explains principal vs secondary actions, modal opening behavior, and priority via codetable or mapped WDO attribute. Lists validation rules for subject/action mappings, list and Context_Parallel indexing, placeholder counts, and STRING-type mapped priority/comment fields. Notes key APIs for resolving task strings, task admin/history/deadlines, and that running a manual activity creates and allocates a task.

children.title: Allocation strategy
Prerequisites
children.summary: This section states the prerequisite for manual activity allocation strategies of type Function: the allocation functions must be Cúram business methods with a specific signature. It points readers to section 1.7 Automatic for the input mappings and metadata for those formal parameters. It also introduces allocation-strategy metadata, including the type field, which can be function, classic rules, CER rules, or target.

children.title: Metadata
Function Allocation Strategy
children.summary: Explains allocation strategy identifiers and, specifically, a function allocation strategy for workflow manual activities. The identifier stores the fully qualified allocation function name when type="function" (or a ruleset/target ID for other types). Includes an XML example showing `<allocation-strategy type="function">` with `function-mappings`, where formal parameters map workflow data objects and task/runtime fields into base types and structs such as `TaskDetails` and `PersonDetails`.

children.title: Classic Rules Allocation
children.summary: Describes classic rules-based allocation for a manual activity using `<allocation-strategy type="rule">` with a ruleset identifier (e.g., `PRODUCT_1`). Shows `ruleset-mappings` that map source workflow data (`TaskCreateDetails.caseID`, `TaskCreateDetails.concernRoleID`) to target rule object attributes (`TaskDetails.caseID`, `TaskDetails.concernRoleID`). Useful for understanding how task allocation inputs are bound to a ruleset in Cúram workflow metadata.

children.title: CER Rules Allocation
children.summary: Defines CER Rules Allocation for workflow manual activities. It explains how allocation rulesets map workflow data object attributes to rules data object attributes at runtime using `ruleset-mappings` and individual `rdo-mapping` entries. A valid mapping includes `source-attribute` (workflow data object name + attribute) and `target-attribute` (rules data object name + attribute). Example XML shows an `allocation-strategy` of type “curam express rule” with `cer-set-mappings` and multiple `cer-class-mapping` elements.

children.title: Target Allocation Strategy
children.summary: Describes the target allocation strategy for a manual activity, including how `allocation-strategy type="target"` is configured with an identifier. Explains `cer-set-mappings` as the set of mappings for the CER rule set referenced by the allocation identifier, and notes the primary class must include a `targets` attribute used by the workflow engine to determine allocation targets. Details `cer-class-mapping`, `source-attribute`, and `target-attribute`, including supported attribute types and how workflow data objects map to CER class attributes.

Defines metadata for manual-activity allocation strategies in Cúram workflow. A strategy has a type and identifier: function uses a fully qualified allocation function name, rule/CER use a ruleset ID, and target uses an allocation target ID. It also documents Context_Task.taskID as the task-created context WDO. Covers function-mappings for formal parameter input binding, classic rules via ruleset-mappings/rdo-mapping, CER rules via cer-set-mappings/cer-class-mapping with a required primary class containing targets, and direct target allocation.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists validation rules for manual task allocation strategies. It requires an allocation strategy to be defined, then details checks for function, classic rule, and CER rule types: valid existing functions and return types, correct workflow data object input mappings and loop constraints, valid rulesets and CER class requirements, matching attribute types, required `targets` attributes, no duplicate CER target mappings, and proper specified/not-specified input mappings.

children.title: Code
children.summary: Explains workflow task allocation code requirements. Allocation functions for manual/decision activities must return `curam.util.workflow.struct.AllocationTargetList` and generally should not throw exceptions, since failures trigger Workflow Error Handling and possible retries. Describes implementation of `curam.util.workflow.impl.WorkResolver`, configured via `curam.custom.workflow.workresolver`, which the engine uses to allocate tasks. Notes `resolveWork` is overloaded for different allocation strategy output formats, but custom implementations should keep business logic consistent. Includes rules for classic/CER mappings and use of loop/parallel context data objects.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains runtime behavior for manual activity allocation. The workflow engine reads the task’s allocation strategy from metadata and derives allocation targets differently for function, classic rule, CER rule, and target strategies. It describes how input mappings, rulesets, or CER class mappings are processed, and notes that target strategy uses the metadata value directly. It also mentions that a failure allocation strategy can be defined for a workflow process.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: Explains workflow task allocation fallback behavior when no allocation targets are returned: the workflow engine calls the configured work resolver, then checks task assignment and, if still unassigned, routes the task to the default work queue. If a task ends up assigned to exactly one user, it may be automatically added to that user’s My Tasks list depending on a property. Also notes that the Context_Task WDO is available for allocation function, classic, and CER ruleset mappings, and introduces business object associations for linking tasks to application entities like Case and Concern.

Defines manual activity allocation strategies in Cúram workflow: function, classic rules, CER rules, and target. Covers required metadata, input/ruleset/CER class mappings, and the Context_Task.taskID WDO. Notes function allocation methods must be Cúram business methods returning AllocationTargetList, and indexed list/parallel mappings have loop/parallel constraints. Lists validation rules for strategy types and mapping consistency. Also explains runtime processing and work resolver implementation (WorkResolver/DefaultWorkResolverAdapter) plus fallback behavior when no targets are returned.

children.title: Business Object Associations
Metadata
children.summary: Defines workflow metadata for manual activities, showing how allocation, task assignment, and business object links are configured. It explains that if allocation returns no targets, the workflow uses the work resolver; if the task still has no assignment, it falls back to the default work queue. It also notes automatic addition to a user’s My Tasks list when only one user is assigned and the relevant flag is enabled. Includes Context_Task WDO availability and an example biz-object-associations XML mapping task data to application entities.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: Validation rules for manual activity business object associations: the business object type must be a valid BusinessObjectType code-table value, and the mapped workflow data object attribute must be assignable to LONG because it represents a unique identifier. Indexed list values like PersonDetailsList[Context_Loop.loopCount] require the WDO to be a list and the activity to be inside a loop. Context_Parallel mappings require a Parallel manual activity. Parallel List WDO indexed mappings must be in a Parallel Activity and reference the Parallel Activity List WDO.

children.title: Code
children.summary: This section covers the “Code” for manual activity business object associations in Cúram Workflow. It points to the BusinessObjectAssociationAdmin API and notes that its Javadoc documents available functions for manipulating associations. Relevant nearby details include the XML tags and validations for biz-object-associations, biz-object-association, and biz-object-type, including rules for valid codetable values and workflow data object mappings to unique identifiers.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains runtime behavior for manual activities and decision activities. It notes that business object associations do not affect execution, but the workflow engine records them in BizObjAssociation using the business object type, mapped value, and newly created task ID. Manual activities reuse the event wait mechanism to notify completion, and Context_Task WDO is available in deadline, allocation, classic, CER, and event match mappings. It then introduces Decision as a manual activity specialization for metadata-driven simple questions, supporting multiple choice and single-field answers.
