Covers manual activity business object associations in Workflow. Explains how tasks can be linked to application entities via `biz-object-associations`, `biz-object-association`, and `biz-object-type` metadata, with XML examples mapping WDO attributes to unique identifier values. Lists validation rules: business object type must be from `BusinessObjectType`, mapped WDO values must be LONG-compatible, and indexed mappings require loop or parallel context as appropriate. Also points to the `BusinessObjectAssociationAdmin` API and its Javadoc for managing associations.

children.title: Event Wait
Prerequisites
children.summary: This section covers prerequisites for manual activity event-wait behavior. It notes that business object associations do not affect runtime execution, but the workflow engine records BizObjAssociation entries using the business object type, mapped value, and task identifier. It explains that manual activities reuse the event-wait mechanism because user action is required to continue. It also points to related event-wait metadata and states that the Context_Task WDO is available in input mappings and event match data for manual activities.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: Context_Task is a workflow data object available in manual activity event-wait input mappings, including deadline functions, allocation function mappings, classic/CER rule mappings, and event match data. The section explains that manual activities reuse the event-wait mechanism because they depend on user action before completion. It also notes that business object associations are recorded by the workflow engine but do not affect manual activity execution.

Manual activities reuse the event-wait mechanism because a user action is needed before completion. Business object associations do not affect execution, but the workflow engine records BizObjAssociation entries using the business object type, mapped WDO value, and newly created task ID. The Context_Task workflow data object is available in manual activity event-wait mappings, including deadline functions, allocation/classic/CER rule inputs, and event match data. The section points readers to the general Event Wait metadata details.

Manual activities model human intervention in workflow, creating inbox tasks with parameterized localized messages, action links to pages, principal/secondary actions, modal behavior, link parameters, multiple-occurring actions, priority, and initial comments. They support task allocation via function, classic rules, CER rules, or fixed target, with runtime resolution through WorkResolver and fallback assignment if no targets are returned. The section also covers task deadlines/reminders, Context_Task availability, and business object associations that link tasks to application entities with validation rules and admin API references.

children.title: 1.10 Decision
Prerequisites
children.summary: This section explains prerequisites for decision activities in Cúram workflow. It points readers to the shared base activity details and common workflow metadata (such as allocation strategy, task subject, and task deadline). It also introduces decision activity task behavior: one associated task action, no action page required, and optional secondary actions for supplementary links. A metadata example shows how decision activity messages and parameters are defined.

children.title: Task Details
Metadata
children.summary: Defines decision activity metadata in Cúram workflow. It explains that decision activities notify users, create one associated task action with no action page, and may include secondary actions linking to supplementary pages. The section shows XML examples for allocation-strategy, parameterized task message, decision-action message, secondary-actions, and deadline. It also notes that common workflow metadata is shared with manual activities and points to base activity and manual activity references for shared details.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: Validates decision activity configuration: required subject text, allocation strategy, optional deadline handler, and secondary actions. Checks that mapped workflow data object attributes exist and match types, placeholder counts match parameters, and indexed list/parallel references are only used in the correct loop, Parallel decision, or Parallel Activity contexts. Also requires valid business methods/rulesets, non-blank page links/messages, valid secondary action parameter mappings, and proper multiple-occurring action setup using a valid List WDO with at least one linked attribute.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Describes how a decision activity runs at runtime: the workflow engine creates the task, snapshots required workflow data for subject/action and secondary message/link parameters, starts the allocation strategy to assign users, and creates a DECISION.MADE event wait using the task ID. The workflow pauses until that event is raised. Also outlines supported question formats (multiple choice and free text) and how auto-generated decision pages use question metadata to render the question and answer options.

Defines decision activity task details in Cúram workflow. Decision activities create one task action with no action page plus optional secondary actions linking to supporting pages, and use metadata for allocation strategy, subject/action messages, deadlines, and multiple-occurring actions. Includes examples for parameterized messages, link parameters, and secondary action setup. Validation rules cover required subject, correct placeholder/parameter counts, valid WDO attributes and context-sensitive indexing, valid allocation strategy and deadline handler, nonblank secondary pages/messages, and proper list WDO usage for multiple-occurring actions.

children.title: Question Details
Metadata
Multiple Choice
children.summary: Describes the multiple choice question format for decision activities in Cúram workflow. Explains how the auto-generated decision page uses question metadata, then details the XML structure for question, message, answers, multiple-selection, answer names, answer-text, and choice-output-mapping. Shows how selected answers are passed to the engine and persisted to workflow data object attributes, including selected-value and default Boolean behavior. Includes an example question asking whether a claimant is over 18 with yes/no answer mappings.

children.title: Free Text
children.summary: Defines the Free Text question type in a decision activity. Shows how a parameterized message is defined with localized text and message parameters, and how the free-text answer is typed (for example INT32) and mapped back to a workflow data object via wdo-attribute. Notes that Boolean workflow attributes default to false if not set. Includes validation rules: question text and answer format are required, and the number of text placeholders must match the mapped workflow attributes.

Describes decision activity question metadata used to auto-generate the decision page. Covers multiple choice questions: localized message text with parameters, answer lists, single/multi-select flag, valid Java answer names, and choice-output-mapping to persist selected values to workflow data object attributes, with Boolean defaults to true. Also covers free text questions: parameterized localized prompts, typed answer values, wdo-attribute mapping, and validation that required text/answer format and placeholder counts match.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists validation rules for decision activity questions, especially free-text questions. It states that both the answer format and question text are required, the number of placeholders in question text must match the number of mapped workflow data object attributes across locales, and indexed list items used as question parameters must be handled consistently in the workflow. Useful if you need constraints on how decision activity question text and parameters must be defined.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Describes runtime behavior and context data for decision activities. When a decision answer is supplied, a DECISION.MADE event is raised using the decision task identifier, and the workflow event handler advances the process. Free-text answers are mapped to the configured workflow data object attribute. It also explains Context_Decision usage in conditions: free-text exposes Context_Decision.value, while multiple-choice answers populate Boolean attributes for each option to indicate selection.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: Describes the Context_Decision workflow data object for decision activity outcomes. It can be used in data item or function conditions, especially transition conditions from a decision activity. For free text answers, Context_Decision.value holds the entered text and can map to a workflow data object attribute. For multiple choice answers, the context WDO exposes Boolean attributes for each answer option (for example, yesAnswer) indicating which choice was selected.

Covers decision activity question details and runtime behavior. Explains metadata for multiple-choice and free-text questions, including parameterized localized prompts, answer lists, Java-valid answer names, selection flags, output mapping to workflow data objects, and free-text type mapping. Lists validations for required question/answer format, placeholder counts, indexed list/parallel parameters, and data type consistency. Also describes runtime DECISION.MADE handling and the Context_Decision WDO for transition conditions, with Boolean choice attributes and free-text value access.

Decision activities are a manual-activity specialization for metadata-driven user questions. They create one task action with no action page, optional secondary actions, and use allocation strategy, subject/action text, and deadline metadata like manual activities. Supported question types are multiple choice and free text, with localized parameterized prompts, answer/output mapping, and validation rules for placeholders, WDO types, and list/parallel indexing. Runtime raises DECISION.MADE; Context_Decision exposes answer values/flags for transition conditions.

children.title: 1.11 Subflow
Prerequisites
children.summary: Prerequisites for the Subflow activity. It states that the base activity details in section 1.6 apply to subflows, and explains that a subflow starts another named workflow process as part of a parent process. The process name must exactly match and the released version is enacted. Subflows may run synchronously, blocking the parent branch until completion, or asynchronously, where the parent continues immediately and ignores the subflow outcome.

children.title: Subflow Process
Metadata
children.summary: Defines the subflow activity metadata for Cúram Workflow. It explains that a subflow starts another named workflow process as part of a parent process, either synchronously (parent waits) or asynchronously (parent continues immediately). The metadata example shows the `<subflow workflow-process="ApproveCase" synchronous="true"/>` element. It also notes that the workflow process name is case-sensitive and must exactly match the released process to be started.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists subflow activity validations and input mappings. It states that a workflow process must be specified for the subflow activity, and that the selected workflow process must have at least one released version. It also explains how parent process workflow data objects are mapped into subflow enactment data via `<input-mappings>`, with XML examples. A note adds that if the subflow has no attributes required at enactment, no input mappings are needed.

Explains the Subflow activity in Cúram Workflow: how a parent process can start another named workflow as a subflow, either synchronously (parent waits) or asynchronously (parent continues). Notes that the subflow must specify the released workflow process by exact, case-sensitive name, with example metadata `<subflow workflow-process="ApproveCase" synchronous="true"/>`. Also frames subflows as a way to split large processes into reusable, independently changeable components.

children.title: Input Mappings
Metadata
children.summary: Defines subflow activity metadata for input mappings. Explains how parent process workflow data objects map to subflow enactment data via `<input-mappings>` and `<mapping>` elements using source-attribute and target-attribute with WDO names and attribute names. Notes the subflow can be synchronous or asynchronous relative to the parent. Validations require a workflow process to be specified and to have at least one released version. Also states that if the subflow has required-at-enactment workflow data object attributes, no input mappings are needed.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section defines subflow input and output mappings for workflow data objects. It explains the mapping tags, including source-attribute, target-attribute, wdo-name, and name, and how parent-process attributes populate subflow attributes at enactment. Key validations cover required enactment attributes, type compatibility, indexed list items used in loops, and list-to-list mapping rules. It also notes that output mappings apply only to synchronous subflow activities, where data is returned to the parent after the subflow completes.

Defines how a subflow receives enactment data from the parent process via `<input-mappings>` and `<mapping>` elements. Uses `source-attribute` and `target-attribute` with WDO names/attribute names to map parent workflow data to subflow data, including list and indexed loop-item cases. Notes subflows may be synchronous or asynchronous, but input mappings are for data passed at start. Validations require a specified workflow process with at least one released version; if the subflow has required-at-enactment attributes, no input mappings are needed.

children.title: Output Mappings
Metadata
children.summary: This section defines subflow input/output mapping metadata for workflow data objects. It explains mapping tags such as mapping, source-attribute, target-attribute, source/target-attribute, wdo-name, and name, and how they identify parent and subflow attributes. It also lists validation rules: required enactment attributes must be mapped, data types must match or be assignable, and list items require list workflow data objects and loop context. Output mappings apply only to synchronous subflows.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section describes subflow output mappings, showing how data is copied from a completed subprocess back to the parent process. It explains the roles of output-mappings, mapping, source-attribute, and target-attribute, including use with list workflow data objects. The validations state that the source and target attributes must be valid in their process contexts, and the parent target attribute’s type must match or be assignable from the subflow source attribute’s type.

Defines subflow output mappings for synchronous subflows, showing how data is copied from a completed subflow back to the parent process. Describes the mapping tags used to identify source and target workflow data object attributes, including list WDO support. Notes that output mappings are only applicable to synchronous subflows; asynchronous subflows do not use them. Validation requires both attributes to be valid in their process contexts, and the parent target attribute’s type must match or be assignable from the subflow source attribute’s type.

Defines the Subflow activity in Cúram Workflow: a parent process can start another named, released workflow process as a subflow, synchronously (parent waits) or asynchronously (parent continues). Covers metadata for `workflow-process` and `synchronous`, with exact case-sensitive process-name matching. Describes input mappings from parent to subflow using `source-attribute`/`target-attribute`, including list and loop-indexed values, and notes required-at-enactment handling. Also covers synchronous-only output mappings from subflow back to parent and their validation rules.

children.title: 1.12 Loop Begin and Loop End
Prerequisites
children.summary: Introduces loop begin/loop end activities in Cúram workflow. Notes that activities between a paired loop-begin and loop-end repeat until a loop-exit condition is met. Explains how to build the loop with transitions, warns not to connect loop-end back to loop-begin, and states loops must be fully nested to preserve valid block structure. Also distinguishes loop types: while loops test the condition before execution, while do-while loops test after and run at least once.

children.title: Overview
Loop Type
children.summary: This section explains loop type for Cúram workflow loop-begin/loop-end activities. It distinguishes while loops, which test the exit condition before executing the loop and may skip the loop entirely, from do-while loops, which test at the end and always run at least once. It also shows loop metadata syntax using the loop-type attribute. Open this section if you need to understand how loop execution timing is configured in workflow process definitions.

Explains Cúram workflow loop-begin/loop-end activities. Loop begin and end must be paired in a valid block structure, with transitions from loop-begin through repeated activities to loop-end; do not connect loop-end back to the start. Loops use a loop-exit condition and can be nested only when fully nested. Also introduces loop type metadata, distinguishing while loops (test before execution, may skip entirely) from do-while loops (test after, run at least once).

children.title: Metadata
Loop Begin Activity
children.summary: Defines Cúram workflow loop-begin/loop-end activities and how they work as paired metadata-linked nodes. Explains that activities between them repeat until a loop-exit condition is met, with transitions from loop-begin to the first repeated activity and from the last activity to loop-end. Notes loops must be fully nested (no interleaving) to preserve valid block structure, and warns against creating a transition from loop-end back to the start. Also distinguishes loop types: while (condition tested before execution) and do-while (tested after, runs at least once).

children.title: Loop End Activity
children.summary: Describes the loop-end-activity XML element and its block-endpoint-ref. The block-endpoint-ref links the loop end back to the associated loop-begin-activity so the workflow engine knows where to restart the loop when the exit condition is false after an iteration. Useful for understanding loop control flow and how loops continue execution in workflow definitions.

Defines Cúram workflow loop metadata. Explains paired loop-begin and loop-end activities, how transitions enter the loop and rejoin at loop-end, and that loops must be fully nested with no transition from loop-end back to the start. Covers loop-exit conditions, while vs do-while behavior, and the XML elements loop-type, condition, and block-endpoint-ref. Notes list workflow data objects can use size() and isEmpty() in loop conditions, and block-endpoint-ref links each loop end to its matching begin for engine control flow.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains loop runtime behavior in Workflow Reference. Each activity instance in a loop is tied to a specific iteration so repeated runs do not corrupt process data. Defines when the Context_Loop WDO is available: loop conditions, transitions, input mappings, allocation/deadline handlers, and various text/association parameters for Manual, Decision, and notification activities inside loops. Notes the only listed attribute, Context_Loop.loopCount, which gives the number of loop iterations. Also begins section 1.13 Parallel, introducing runtime parallel activities.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: Explains the Context_Loop workflow data object used for activities inside loops. It is available when defining loop conditions, outgoing transitions, input mappings for automatic/subflow activities, allocation strategy and deadline handler functions, and text/link/question/body parameters for manual, decision, and notification elements within a loop. The only listed attribute is Context_Loop.loopCount, which gives the number of loop iterations. Also introduces parallel activities as wrappers for existing activities to run multiple instances in parallel when the number of paths is not known at design time.

Explains Cúram workflow loop-begin/loop-end activities. Loop begin and loop end must be paired, with transitions from the begin through repeated activities to the end; do not connect the end back to the start. Loops repeat until a loop-exit condition is met and must be fully nested to preserve valid block structure. Covers loop types: while tests before execution, do-while after and runs at least once. Also describes metadata elements loop-type, condition, and block-endpoint-ref, plus list WDO helpers size() and isEmpty() in loop conditions.

children.title: 1.13 Parallel
Prerequisites
children.summary: Prerequisites for parallel activities in Cúram workflow: they rely on the common base activity details and on metadata defined for manual and decision activities. A parallel activity must declare the type of wrapped activity and associate a list workflow data object; the number of items in that list determines how many instances the workflow engine creates at runtime. Supported wrapped activity types are manual-activity and decision-activity.

children.title: Metadata
Generic Metadata for a Parallel Activity
children.summary: Defines the metadata for a parallel activity in Cúram workflow. A parallel activity wraps an existing manual or decision activity and creates multiple task instances in parallel, with the number determined by an associated list workflow data object. It requires base activity metadata plus relevant manual/decision metadata. The section shows XML examples using `<parallel-activity>`, `<list-wdo-name>`, and either `<manual-activity>` or `<decision-activity>`, and explains the `manual-activity/decision-activity` element as the wrapped activity type.

children.title: Metadata for a Parallel Manual Activity
children.summary: Describes the metadata for a Parallel Manual Activity, showing that it uses the same metadata and validation rules as a standard Manual activity. It highlights that Context_Parallel and an indexed item from the Parallel Activity List WDO can be used in mappings. An example illustrates how message parameters, event waits, and business object associations reference EmployerDetailsListWDO[Context_Parallel.occurrenceCount]. Useful for understanding how parallel manual task instances are parameterized and mapped at runtime.

children.title: Metadata for a Parallel Decision Activity
children.summary: This section describes metadata for a Parallel Decision Activity wrapped around a Decision. It states that the metadata and validations are the same as for a standard Decision activity (referenced from section 1.10), so they are not repeated. It shows that the Context_Parallel Workflow Data Object and an indexed Parallel Activity List WDO item can be used in all mappings for a Parallel Decision Activity, with example XML using message parameters and occurrenceCount indexing.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section states the validation rules for a parallel activity. A workflow data object is required; it must be a list-type workflow data object and valid within the containing workflow process definition. It also notes that any other parallel-activity validations are covered in the sections for the activities that a parallel activity can wrap, specifically Manual and Decision.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains runtime behavior of Parallel Activity and Activity Notifications. A parallel activity is executed as multiple wrapped activity instances, one per item in the associated list WDO, effectively within an AND split/join so all instances must complete before the parallel activity finishes. It describes Context_Parallel.occurrenceCount, a zero-based INTEGER used to index the list item for data mapping. Also covers how the workflow engine creates notifications when configured in activity metadata, with delivery via email, inbox alerts, or both.

children.title: Description of Context WDOs
children.summary: Explains the Context_Parallel workflow data object used by Parallel Activities. Each parallel activity instance maps to one item in the parallel activity list WDO, indexed by Context_Parallel.occurrenceCount, a zero-based INTEGER. Indexed items are then used for standard data mappings. The section also notes that parallel activities run as multiple wrapped activity instances within an AND split/join, and introduces the next topic, activity notifications.

This section defines parallel activity metadata in Cúram workflow. A parallel activity wraps either a Manual or Decision activity and creates one task instance per item in an associated list workflow data object. It shows XML structure for `<parallel-activity>`, `<list-wdo-name>`, and `<manual-activity>`/`<decision-activity>`. It explains that wrapped activities use the same metadata and validations as their standard forms, and that `Context_Parallel.occurrenceCount` indexes the list item for mappings. It also states the workflow data object must be a valid list WDO.

Parallel activities wrap either Manual or Decision activities to create multiple task instances in parallel when the number of paths is only known at runtime. The number of instances is driven by an associated list workflow data object, which must be valid for the process definition. The section gives XML metadata examples for `<parallel-activity>`, `<list-wdo-name>`, and wrapped activity types, and shows how `Context_Parallel.occurrenceCount` indexes the list item in mappings, messages, events, and business-object associations.

children.title: 1.14 Activity Notifications
Notification Details
Metadata
children.summary: Explains notification metadata in Cúram workflow. Notifications are human-facing alerts or emails sent when a process step executes, with recipients chosen by the allocation strategy. The section shows XML examples for a notification’s subject, body, actions, link parameters, and multiple-occurring actions, all using parameterized localized text and WDO attributes. It also defines the delivery-mechanism tag, which maps to the application codetable and is used by the configured delivery service, not by workflow logic.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists validation rules for notification configuration. It requires a subject and body, and says all workflow data object attributes used in subject/body mappings must exist and be valid in the containing process definition. It also defines constraints for indexed list items and parallel workflow data objects: list indexes must be used within loops, Context_Parallel only in Parallel activities, and Parallel List WDO indexing must match a Parallel Activity’s list WDO.

children.title: Code
children.summary: This section describes notification and multiple-occurring action validation rules in Cúram workflows, including required allocation strategies, delivery mechanisms, valid rulesets, list/parallel workflow data object constraints, and placeholder-to-attribute matching. It also explains that action pages must be valid Cúram pages with fully populated parameters. Finally, it introduces the LocalizableStringResolver and NotificationStringResolver APIs for resolving localized notification subject, body, and action text during notification delivery.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains workflow runtime notification handling after an activity completes. If the activity has a notification, the engine uses the activity’s allocation strategy to determine recipient users and calls the application’s notification delivery method with notification details. Notes that the application must implement the `NotificationDelivery` callback interface, configured by `curam.custom.notifications.notificationdelivery`. Describes the `deliverNotification` overloads in `curam.util.workflow.impl.NotificationDelivery` for different allocation target formats.

Describes workflow notifications in Cúram: human-facing alerts/emails sent when an activity executes, with recipients chosen by the allocation strategy. Covers notification metadata XML for subject, body, actions, link parameters, multiple-occurring actions, and delivery-mechanism mapping. Lists validation rules for required subject/body, valid WDO references, loop/parallel indexing constraints, placeholder counts, allocation strategy, delivery mechanism, and valid action pages/parameters. Also notes runtime delivery via the application’s NotificationDelivery callback and string resolution APIs for localized notification text.

children.title: Notification Allocation Strategy
Prerequisites
children.summary: Explains workflow notification prerequisites. After an activity completes, the engine checks for an associated notification, resolves recipient users via the activity’s allocation strategy, and invokes the application’s notification delivery method with notification details. The application must implement the NotificationDelivery callback interface, configured by the curam.custom.notifications.notificationdelivery property. The engine calls deliverNotification in curam.util.workflow.impl.NotificationDelivery, passing allocation targets and notification details. The default interface includes overloaded methods to support different allocation target formats.

children.title: Code
children.summary: Explains workflow notification delivery code. The engine invokes a configurable NotificationDelivery callback after an activity completes, passing allocation targets and notification details. DefaultNotificationDeliveryAdapter normalizes different allocation-target formats and lets custom implementations override one method. DefaultNotificationDelivery provides built-in mechanisms via the DELIVERYMECHANISM code table and can be extended safely by overriding selectDeliveryMechanism while preserving standard behavior. The boolean return indicates whether at least one user received the notification; otherwise the workflow engine logs an audit record.

Explains how workflow notifications are allocated and delivered after an activity completes. The engine determines recipients using the activity’s allocation strategy, then calls the application’s NotificationDelivery implementation with notification details and allocation targets. It describes the NotificationDelivery interface, the configurable curam.custom.notifications.notificationdelivery property, and the default adapter that normalizes target formats. It also covers DefaultNotificationDelivery, DELIVERYMECHANISM-based routing, safe extension via selectDeliveryMechanism, and that the returned boolean indicates whether at least one user received the notification.

Defines workflow activity notifications: human-facing alerts/emails created when an activity runs, with recipients chosen by the allocation strategy. Describes notification metadata for localized subject/body, actions, link parameters, and multiple-occurring actions, plus delivery-mechanism codetable mapping. Lists validation rules for required text, valid WDO/loop/parallel references, placeholder counts, action page/parameter validity, and list-WDO constraints. Explains runtime delivery via NotificationDelivery/DefaultNotificationDeliveryAdapter, default routing by DELIVERYMECHANISM, extensibility via selectDeliveryMechanism, and the boolean return meaning at least one user received it.

children.title: 1.15 Transitions
Metadata
children.summary: This section explains workflow transitions as the unidirectional links between activities that control execution order, including branching and synchronization. It frames a process definition as a directed graph from a single start activity to a single end activity. The XML example shows how transitions are defined with from/to activity references and optional conditions using expressions and logical operators.

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists workflow transition validation rules. It states that each transition must connect valid source and target activities within the same workflow definition, and the source and target cannot be the same. It also enforces process-level constraints: the start activity cannot have incoming transitions, the end activity cannot have outgoing transitions, every non-end activity needs at least one inbound transition, and every non-start activity needs at least one outbound transition.

children.title: Runtime Information
children.summary: Explains workflow activity runtime behavior and transaction boundaries between engine and application code. Describes the three activity functions—start(), execute(), complete()—and how the engine uses them, including direct execution for route activities and JMS-based asynchronous execution for automatic activities. Also introduces conditions in workflow flow control: loop and transition conditions, defined as compound expressions over workflow data object attributes and evaluated by parent constructs to determine process flow.

Defines workflow transitions as unidirectional links between activities that control execution order, including branching and synchronization, forming a directed graph from single start to single end activity. Describes transition metadata in XML: required id, from/to activity references, and optional conditions based on workflow data object expressions. Lists validation rules: source/target must be valid and different; start has no incoming transitions; end has no outgoing transitions; all non-end activities need inbound transitions; all non-start activities need outbound transitions.

children.title: 1.16 Conditions
Metadata
children.summary: This section defines workflow condition metadata used by loop begins and transitions. It explains that conditions are lists of expressions combined by conjunction/disjunction, and that loop begins require an exit condition while transitions may optionally have one. It details expression types: data-item comparisons and function expressions using not() or isNothing(). It also documents key attributes such as id, data-item-lhs/rhs, operation, and conjunction, plus valid comparison operators (==, !=, <=, >=, <, >).

children.title: Validations
children.summary: This section lists validation rules for workflow condition expressions. It requires both left and right workflow data object attributes to exist in the process definition, and checks that operators, functions, and conjunctions are valid and supported. It also requires balanced opening/closing brackets across the full condition. Function-specific rules apply: Not() requires a BOOLEAN right-side attribute, while isNothing() requires a non-BOOLEAN attribute.

Defines workflow condition metadata for loops and transitions. Conditions are lists of expressions combined with and/or, with loop begins requiring an exit condition and transitions optionally supporting one. Covers two expression types: data-item comparisons and function expressions using not() or isNothing(). Documents attributes such as id, data-item-lhs/rhs, operation, conjunction, function, and bracket counts. Lists valid comparison operators (==, !=, <=, >=, <, >) and validation rules, including valid workflow data objects, balanced brackets, and function-specific type constraints.

children.title: 1.17 Split/Join
Choice XOR Split
Metadata
children.summary: This section covers metadata rules for workflow condition expressions and split/join behavior. It specifies type compatibility requirements for data item comparisons, constraints on indexed list items, and use of size() and isEmpty() in loop conditions. It then introduces split/join metadata for activities: every split must be matched by a join to preserve block structure, and split/join types can be none, choice (XOR), or parallel (AND).

Introduces workflow split/join metadata and the Choice (XOR) split. Explains that valid process structure requires every split to be matched by a join, and that splits support either parallel work or choosing one path. Defines activity metadata types: none for single transitions, and choice/XOR or parallel/AND as the main split/join types. Relevant for understanding how conditional branching is represented in process definitions.

children.title: Parallel AND split
Metadata
children.summary: Explains workflow metadata for activity splits. Defines the `split` tag as the outgoing transition list resolved when an activity completes, and notes ordering matters for `xor` because the first eligible transition is taken. Describes split types: `none` for a single path, `xor` for exclusive choice, and `and` for parallel execution of all eligible transitions. Also defines `transition-id` and its `idref` reference to a process transition, with an example of a parallel AND split.

Defines workflow activity split metadata, focusing on a parallel AND split example. Explains that `split` lists outgoing transitions resolved when an activity completes, `transition-id` references process transitions via `idref`, and `type="and"` means all eligible transitions are followed in parallel. Also notes `none` means one outgoing path and `xor` means the first eligible transition in order is taken, so transition order matters.

Explains workflow split/join metadata for process definitions. Describes how transitions can branch from an activity and must be matched by a join to preserve valid block structure. Covers the three split/join types: none for a single path, xor/choice for one selected path, and and/parallel for multiple concurrent paths. Shows XML metadata for `<split>`, `<join>`, and `<transition-id idref>`, and notes that XOR follows the first eligible transition in list order.

children.title: 1.18 Workflow Structure
Graph Structure
children.summary: Explains workflow graph structure: activities are vertices and transitions are arcs, allowing graph theory checks before process execution. The section notes that workflow graphs must meet certain criteria for the engine to run them successfully. It highlights a key problem, informal/ad hoc loops, where cycles without transition conditions can cause infinite looping and block structural validation. Cúram instead uses formal loop-begin and loop-end constructs to delimit iteration and detect/prevent ad hoc loops from being released.

children.title: Block Structure
An Analogy for Blocks
children.summary: Explains “blocks” in Cúram workflow using analogies to GOTO, braces, and mathematical brackets. Describes how block-forming elements come in matched start/end pairs, define control-flow regions, and can be validated for structural problems such as a Choice Split feeding a Parallel Join. Introduces supported block types and begins the Choice (XOR) block, which starts at a Choice Split and ends at a Choice Join, allowing only one mutually exclusive outbound path.

children.title: Block Types Supported by Workflow
'Choice' (XOR) Block
children.summary: Explains workflow block structure and introduces the Choice (XOR) block. A Choice block begins with a Choice (XOR) Split and ends with a Choice (XOR) Join, like matching brackets. It allows multiple outbound transitions but only one mutually exclusive path is followed by a process instance. The section also frames blocks as paired start/end elements that enforce control-flow behavior and notes that block-structure issues can be detected by validation before runtime.

children.title: 'Parallel' (AND) Block
children.summary: Explains the Cúram workflow Parallel (AND) block: it begins at a Parallel Split and ends at a Parallel Join, allowing multiple outgoing paths to run in parallel when conditions permit, then synchronizing all paths before continuing. Also notes related Loop Block behavior and introduces structural validation rules for workflows: the process graph must be directed, connected, and acyclic, meaning all activities must be reachable and ad hoc loops are disallowed.

children.title: 'Loop' Block
children.summary: Explains the Cúram Loop Block: it starts at a loop-begin activity and ends at a loop-end activity, enclosing workflow steps that repeat when the loop condition is met. Execution returns to the loop-begin if the condition is true, otherwise it jumps past the loop-end. The section also introduces structural validation rules for workflow graphs, including that processes must be directed, connected, and acyclic, meaning no ad hoc loops via transitions.

Introduces workflow block types and their paired start/end structure. Explains that blocks control flow behavior and can be validated before runtime to catch invalid structures, such as a Choice split feeding a Parallel join and causing a stall. Uses bracket/loop analogies to show that blocks must be matched and nested, not interleaved. Begins describing the Choice (XOR) block: it starts at a Choice Split, ends at a Choice Join, and allows multiple outbound transitions but only one mutually exclusive path is taken.

Introduces workflow “block structure” using GOTO, braces, and bracket analogies. Explains that certain elements—Choice XOR Split/Join, Parallel AND Split, and Loop Begin/End—always come in matched pairs that define control-flow blocks, which can be validated before runtime to catch invalid structures like a Choice split feeding a Parallel join. Begins the Choice (XOR) block: it starts at a Choice Split, ends at a Choice Join, and allows multiple outbound transitions but only one mutually exclusive path is taken.

children.title: Structural Rules
Graph Structure Rules
children.summary: Explains Cúram workflow structural constraints for process validation. A process graph must be directed, connected, and acyclic: transitions are one-way, every activity must be reachable from start to end, and there must be no directed cycles or ad hoc loops. Also reinforces matching block constructs: Choice Split/Join, Parallel (AND) Split/Join, and Loop blocks using loop-begin and loop-end activities. Useful for understanding allowable workflow structure and why invalid designs cannot be executed.

children.title: Block Structure Rules
children.summary: Explains Cúram workflow block structure rules: Choice, Parallel, and Loop blocks must be properly matched with corresponding end constructs, all split paths must rejoin at the correct join, and blocks may be nested but not interleaved. It gives rationale about readability, synchronization, and avoiding engine ambiguity, including an example of invalid join behavior across loop boundaries. It also notes that valid workflows must be directed, connected, acyclic, and block-structured, with structural validations performed by the PDT in a defined order.

Defines Cúram workflow structural validation rules. A valid process must be a directed, connected, acyclic graph: transitions are one-way, every activity must be reachable from start to end, and ad hoc loops are forbidden. It also enforces proper block structure: Choice Split/Join, Parallel Split/Join, and Loop blocks must be correctly matched, with nested blocks allowed but not interleaved. Useful for checking whether a workflow design is structurally valid and executable.

children.title: Validations
Simple Syntactic Checks
children.summary: This section lists workflow structural validation rules. It covers simple syntactic checks on activity joins/splits, including inbound/outbound transition counts and required NONE settings for single transitions and Parallel activities. It then explains graph checks, requiring the workflow to be connected, acyclic (except loop begin/end constructs), and to terminate correctly at the end activity. Finally, it introduces block checks for correct block structure, defining start/end constructs such as Start/End, Loop Begin/End, Parallel split/join, and Choice split/join.

children.title: Graph Checks
children.summary: This section explains workflow structural validation rules. It covers simple syntactic checks for activity joins/splits, including requirements for inbound/outbound transitions and Parallel activity constraints. It then describes graph checks to ensure the workflow is a connected, directed acyclic graph where every activity lies on a path from start to end, with loop-begin/loop-end cycles allowed. Finally, it introduces block checks for proper block structure, defining block-start and block-end constructs and requiring matching pairs such as Loop Begin/End.

children.title: Block Checks
children.summary: This section describes block checks for workflow structural validation. It explains the block-structured flow graph rules, defines block-start and block-end constructs (Start Process, Loop Begin/End, Parallel AND Split/Join, Choice XOR Split/Join), and gives the key validation shown here: every block start must have a corresponding block end (for example, a Loop Begin must pair with a Loop End). Useful for understanding how Cúram verifies proper block nesting in process definitions.

This section introduces Cúram workflow structural validations. It says valid workflows must be directed, connected, acyclic, and block-structured, and that validations run in order. It covers simple syntactic checks for joins/splits and Parallel activity constraints, graph checks for connectivity, acyclicity, and correct termination at the end activity, and block checks defining block-start/end constructs (Start/End, Loop Begin/End, Parallel and Choice splits/joins) with required matching pairs.

Explains how Cúram workflow structure is validated as a graph plus matched control-flow blocks. Activities are vertices and transitions are arcs; the engine requires a directed, connected, acyclic graph with no ad hoc loops. It defines Choice (XOR), Parallel (AND), and Loop blocks, each with required start/end pairs, and notes blocks may nest but not interleave. Also outlines validation order: syntactic split/join checks, graph checks for connectivity/cycles/termination, and block checks for matching constructs and correct synchronization.

children.title: 1.19 Workflow Web Services
Exposing a workflow web service
Process Enactment
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram workflow processes can be exposed as web services and enacted by BPEL-orchestrated processes. It notes that Cúram is not a full BPEL engine, but can participate via a single document-oriented web service BPO fronting the workflow enactment API. It covers how to mark a process definition as a web service in PDT or metadata, rebuild the server/EARs, and access the WSDL when the web services EAR is deployed.

children.title: Process completion callback
children.summary: Describes the process completion callback for Cúram workflow web services. External systems that start a workflow may need a callback when the process completes, possibly with output data. The callback web service is defined in process metadata (via PDT or directly), must first be registered as a Cúram outbound web service connector, and must conform to the workflow’s callback port type. The section also introduces the WSDL “Callback Port Type,” named `<ProcessName>Complete`, intended for implementation by the BPEL or external system.

This section explains how Cúram workflow processes can be exposed as web services for BPEL-orchestrated systems. It notes that Cúram is not a full BPEL engine, but can be enacted via a single document-oriented web service BPO that fronts the workflow enactment API. It covers marking a process as a web service in PDT or metadata, rebuilding the server/EARs, and accessing the WSDL after the web services EAR is deployed, using the process name as the service name.

children.title: Invocation from BPEL processes
children.summary: Explains how Cúram workflow WSDL supports BPEL-enacted processes. The workflow WSDL includes a non-implemented callback port type named <ProcessName>Complete, which defines the interface the BPEL process must implement for process-completion notification. It also notes that the callback web service must be configured in process metadata and registered as a Cúram outbound web service connector. The section is mainly about using WSDL details to integrate BPEL processes with Cúram workflow enactment and completion callbacks.

Cúram workflows can interoperate with BPEL by exposing a workflow process as a web service that BPEL can start and receive completion notification from. The service is backed by one document-oriented BPO fronting the workflow enactment API; customize that single BPO for all workflow web services. Mark the process as a web service in PDT or metadata, rebuild the server/EARs, and use the process name as the WSDL service name. The WSDL also includes a <ProcessName>Complete callback port type for external/BPEL completion callbacks.

children.title: 1.20 File Locations
Workflow Process Definition Files
Customizing Workflow Process Definition Files
Creating New Workflow Process Definition Files
children.summary: Explains how to create and customize workflow process definition XML files in Cúram. New files must be placed in the workflow subdirectory of `...\EJBServer\components\custom`, usually created with the PDT and exported there. The section also notes the strict filename convention `ProcessName_vVersion.xml`, automatic import during `build database`, import precedence by `COMPONENT_ORDER_PRECEDENCE`, validation rules, and that only one unreleased version can be imported. It also briefly introduces event definition files and their XML-based role in application events.

children.title: Changing An Existing Workflow Process Definition File
children.summary: This section explains how to change an existing workflow process definition file in Cúram. Use the PDT to open the latest version, create a new version, make changes, validate it, and release the workflow. Then export the newly released definition and place it in the workflow subdirectory under `...\EJBServer\components\custom`. It also reinforces workflow file conventions and import behavior, including automatic database build import and strict validation requirements.

Explains how Cúram workflow process definition XML files are customized and managed. Files in each component’s workflow directory are auto-imported during build database, must follow `ProcessName_vVersion.xml`, and are validated against name/version; import precedence follows `COMPONENT_ORDER_PRECEDENCE`, and only one unreleased version can be imported. New definitions are created/exported with the PDT into `...\EJBServer\components\custom\workflow`; existing ones are modified by creating a new version, validating, releasing, then exporting it there. Also briefly introduces XML-based event definition files (`.evx`).

Workflow process definitions are stored as XML files in each component’s workflow subdirectory (for example, `...\EJBServer\components\core\workflow`) and are loaded into the database via the standard build database target. Both released and unreleased definitions can be imported from these source locations. This section explains the file-location pattern for workflow-related source files and how exported/admin-created workflow artifacts are externalized for build or install time.

children.title: Event Definition Files
children.summary: This section explains Cúram event definition files (.evx), which define event classes and event types used for loose coupling between modules. It describes how events are raised by application code and received by registered listeners/handlers. It also covers where event files are stored and how they fit into the application’s event mechanism. Useful if you need to create, locate, or understand XML-based event definitions and their role in module communication.

Describes where workflow and event XML files live and how they are loaded. Workflow process definitions are stored in component-specific `...\EJBServer\components\<component>\workflow` folders, imported by the standard build database target, and must follow `ProcessName_vVersion.xml` naming with precedence controlled by `COMPONENT_ORDER_PRECEDENCE`. It also explains how to create/change workflow definitions via PDT and place custom versions under `...\EJBServer\components\custom\workflow`. Event definition files (`.evx`) are XML files that define event classes/types for application event handling and loose coupling.

children.title: 1.21 Configuration
Application Properties
children.summary: Lists workflow-wide application properties configurable in Application.prx. Includes custom WorkResolver class, automatic addition of single-user tasks to My Tasks, custom NotificationDelivery class, and a flag to disable WDOValuesHistory auditing before activity execution. Each entry notes purpose, type, default value, and relevant cross-references.

Explains workflow-wide configuration options in Application.prx. Most workflow settings are process-definition specific, but this section documents four application properties: custom WorkResolver class, automatic addition of single-user tasks to a user’s My Tasks list, custom NotificationDelivery class, and disabling WDOValuesHistory auditing before activity execution. Each property includes purpose, type, default value, and cross-references to related allocation/notification topics.

children.title: 1.22 JMSLite
What JMSLite Does
children.summary: JMSLite is a lightweight JMS server for IDE-based Cúram workflow testing. It supports only transactional point-to-point messaging with ACID transactions across the application database and workflow queue destinations, but not custom queues or publish-subscribe topics. It enables asynchronous, non-blocking workflow APIs so behavior matches application-server production, helping expose temporal-coupling bugs early. The section also explains that JMSLite polls queues, unpacks messages, and calls the IDE test RMI server (StartServer), running as a thread when StartServer starts.

children.title: Why JMSLite?
children.summary: JMSLite is a lightweight JMS server for IDE-based Cúram workflow testing. It supports only transactional point-to-point messaging needed for workflow queues, including ACID transactions with the application database, but not custom queues or topics. It enables asynchronous, non-blocking workflow calls so behavior matches production application-server deployment and helps expose timing bugs early. The section explains that JMSLite polls queues, forwards messages to the IDE testing RMI server (StartServer), and runs as a thread when StartServer starts.

children.title: Using JMSLite
children.summary: Explains JMSLite as the JMS server used for IDE-based Cúram workflow testing. It supports only transactional point-to-point messaging, ACID transactions with the application database and workflow queues, and does not support custom queues or publish-subscribe topics. JMSLite makes workflow APIs non-blocking and asynchronous, matching production behavior to expose timing/coupling bugs early. It also describes operation: the server polls queues, unpacks messages, and invokes the IDE RMI StartServer, running as a thread when StartServer starts.

children.title: Debugging workflows
children.summary: This section explains how to debug workflow methods in Cúram. Because the workflow engine runs on the RMI server and may invoke application methods asynchronously, developers should start StartServer in debug mode, set breakpoints in the target application method, and run the workflow-enacting method to hit them. It lists affected methods: Automatic Activity, Work Allocation Functions, Notification Delivery, and Work Resolver methods. It then begins the Inbox and Task Management area, introducing task lists such as My Open, Deferred, Available, Query Results, Work Queue, and notifications.

JMSLite is Cúram’s lightweight JMS server for IDE-based workflow testing, running alongside StartServer without requiring deployment to an EJB server. It supports only transactional point-to-point messaging, with ACID transactions across the application database and workflow queues, not custom queues or topics. By making workflow APIs asynchronous and non-blocking, it mirrors production behavior and helps expose timing/temporal-coupling bugs early. JMSLite polls queues, unpacks messages, and forwards them to the IDE RMI server as a StartServer thread.

children.title: 1.23 Inbox and Task Management
Inbox Configuration
Inbox List Sizes Configuration Settings
children.summary: Describes Inbox and Task Management configuration, focusing on Inbox list size settings. It lists the main task views available in the Inbox: My Open Tasks, My Deferred Tasks, Available Tasks, Task Query Search Results, Work Queue Tasks, and the notifications list. It also frames these lists as part of Cúram WMS task management for tracking work generated by workflow activities.

children.title: Get Next Task Configuration Settings
children.summary: Describes Inbox “Get Next Task” shortcut functions and how their task-selection algorithm is configured in Application.prx. Covers four retrieval options: next available task, preferred org unit, preferred queue, and chosen queue. Also notes related Inbox list size settings that limit My Open Tasks, Deferred Tasks, Available Tasks, Task Query Search, Work Queue Tasks, and My Notifications, with default max sizes of 100 and overflow messages when results exceed the limit.

children.title: Task Redirection and Allocation Blocking Settings
children.summary: This section explains Task Preferences controls for task redirection and allocation blocking. It defines how users can redirect tasks to another user, org unit, position, job, or work queue for a set period, or block task allocation to themselves for a period. It also lists security identifiers that enable viewing, creating, and clearing these settings for task redirection and task allocation blocking, allowing these Inbox features to be restricted per user.

Cúram WMS Inbox configuration covers task list sizing, “Get Next Task” retrieval behavior, and task preference controls. It defines configurable max sizes for Inbox task lists (My Open, Deferred, Available, Task Query Search, Work Queue Tasks) and My Notifications, with default limits of 100 and overflow warnings. It also documents shortcut task selection options and priority-based retrieval settings in Application.prx. Finally, it explains task redirection and allocation blocking, including security identifiers needed to view, create, or clear these settings.

children.title: Inbox Customization
How to customize the Inbox
Customizing the default implementation
children.summary: Explains how to customize the default InboxActions getNextTask implementation in Cúram Workflow. It shows that the custom class must extend InboxActionsImpl rather than directly implement the interface, to avoid upgrade-time compile issues if new methods are added. The section includes example code for a custom CustomInboxActionsImpl, a Guice Module binding InboxActions to the custom class, and the need to register that module in the ModuleClassName database table (or DMX file) so the application uses the customized behavior after redeployment.

Explains how to customize Inbox behavior in Cúram Workflow, specifically the InboxActionsImpl.getNextTask hook. The section shows that custom code should extend InboxActionsImpl, not implement the InboxActions interface directly, to avoid upgrade compile issues if new methods are added. It provides example custom class and Guice module binding InboxActions to the custom implementation, and notes that the module class must be registered in the ModuleClassName database table or DMX file so the customized behavior is used after redeployment.

Describes how to customize Cúram Workflow Inbox behavior via Guice, focusing on InboxActionsImpl.getNextTask. Explains that custom code should extend the default implementation class, not implement the InboxActions interface directly, to avoid upgrade breakage if new methods are added. Shows example custom class and Guice module binding InboxActions to it, and notes the module class must be registered in the ModuleClassName table or DMX file so the custom implementation is used after redeployment.

Covers Cúram WMS inbox/task management configuration and customization. Key topics: inbox list size limits for task and notification views via Application.prx properties (default 100, overflow warnings); “Get Next Task” retrieval behavior, including priority-based ordering and task priority sequence; task redirection and allocation blocking with security identifiers controlling view/create/clear actions; and Guice-based customization of InboxActions, especially getNextTask, via subclassing default implementations and binding them in a registered module.

Overview of the Cúram Workflow Reference for WMS. It explains the document structure and then covers the main workflow areas: process definition metadata and lifecycle, WDOs and data flow, process enactment via API or events, activity types (base, automatic, event wait, manual, decision, subflow, loop, parallel), activity notifications, flow control (transitions, conditions, split/join), workflow structure validation, web services, file locations, configuration, JMSLite, and inbox/task customization. Later pages include notices and legal information.
